coredata.dc:title: Mechano-thermochemical synthesis of chiral multicentre fluorescent MOFs for chiral VOCs detection and adsorption
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jiaqiang
Su, Xin
Xu, Yan
Tang, Weiwei
Gong, Junbo
coredata.dc:description: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in wastewater pose a serious threat to human health and the environment. With the increasing demand for chiral pharmaceuticals and the development of the chiral industry, the identification, detection and removal of chiral VOCs has become an urgent challenge. Here, we have constructed chiral fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) composites for the first time using single chiral amino acids as a chiral source and in situ carbonation of precursors, integrating both chiral and heterologous multiluminescent centres in the composite system. The blue-green-red luminescence system is composed of heterologous multi-luminescent centres, i.e. ligands, carbon quantum dots and rare earth metals. This heterologous nature allows efficient and accurate qualitative identification of VOCs species and quantitative detection of chiral VOCs concentrations. In particular, its monochiral structure enables enantiomeric excess values detection of chiral enantiomeric mixtures. In addition, the solvent-free synthesis process with competitive coordination of amino acids successfully constructed the multilevel pore structure of the composites, which effectively increased the adsorption rate of the materials by 30 times. The present work provides an efficient method to introduce chirality into multi-centre luminescent MOFs systems, and provides a guiding direction and material design ideas for chiral pollutant detection and removal technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mechano-thermochemical synthesis
Metal-organic frameworks
Chirality
Multicentre fluorescence
Enantioselective recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Qualitative detection of VOCs in solution
Quantitative detection of chiral VOCs solutions
Determination of the ee values of chiral enantiomeric mixtures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The classic method to chiral introduction in MOFs and (b) CDs encapsulated in MOFs. (c) One-step solvent-free synthesis of MOF composite functional materials integrating heterogeneous multiluminescent centres and chirality for recognition and removal of chiral VOCs.

Structural and morphological characterisation of mechano-thermochemically synthesised MOFs composites and their comparison samples. (a) PXRD patterns of MOFs composites and their comparison samples; (b) SEM images and (c) TEM images of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66; (d) FTIR spectra of MOFs composites, comparison samples and some raw materials; (e) nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (f) pore size distributions of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, ST-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 and classical ST-UiO-66. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the full survey (g), Zr 3d (h), Eu 3d (i), C 1 s (j), O 1 s (k), N 1 s (l) of ST-UiO-66 (top) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (bottom), respectively.

Photoluminescence properties of the composites and their comparison materials. Fluorescence excitation (PLE) and fluorescence emission (PL) spectra of (a) MT-UiO-66, (b) MT-CDs@UiO-66, and (c) MT-Eu@UiO-66. (d) Excitation dependent PL spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 with excitation wavelengths from 240 nm to 500 nm. (e) Photographs of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 under sunlight and different excitation wavelengths, respectively. The corresponding CIE (x, y) emission colour coordinate plots of the PL spectra of (f) MT-UiO-66, MT-CDs@UiO-66, MT-Eu@UiO-66 and (g) MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66.
The CPL spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 with D-Phe (top) and L-Phe (bottom) as precursors.

Fluorescence detection of VOCs by MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 dispersion. (a) Structural formulae and names of 14 VOCs to be detected. (b) Emission spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 dispersion after addition of different VOCs (λex = 310 nm). (c) Photographs of the corresponding solutions irradiated at excitation wavelengths of 254 nm, 302 nm and 365 nm (from top to bottom). (d) Peak values of the emission peaks originating from the ligand (338 nm) and metal Eu (616 nm) in the emission spectra. (e) Qualitative detection of unknown VOCs.

Quantitative detection and sensing mechanism of chiral enantiomers of VOCs. Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of (a) R-phenylethylamine ((+)-PEA) and (b) S-phenylethylamine ((-)-PEA), the insets are top-down photographs of the corresponding systems under UV lamps at 254 nm, 302 nm and 365 nm. (c) Linear fitting of the fluorescence emission spectra of the peaks at 388 nm and at 616 nm to the enantiomeric concentration. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra corresponding to different ee of (+)-PEA. (e) Linear fits of peak fluorescence emission spectra at 388 nm and 616 nm to ee of (+)-PEA. (f) Excitation and emission spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 and UV–vis spectra of VOCs.

Comparison of adsorption properties of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, ST-Eu@UiO-66(Phe), and ST-UiO-66 and differences in adsorption of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 for chiral enantiomers. (a-c) Photographs and (d-f) UV–vis spectra of rhodamine B in the presence of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe) and ST-UiO-66 over time. (g) Plot of the efficiency of rhodamine B removal by MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe) and ST-UiO-66.(h) Time-adsorption capacity plots and (i) pseudo-second order model fitting curves of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 adsorption of chiral enantiomers ((+)-PEA and (-)-PEA).
Carbon dots are introduced into zeolite imidazole frameworks (ZIFs). (a) PXRD patterns and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of MT-CDs@ZIFs and MT-ZIFs with different amounts of L-His addition.
Evaluation of the Linearity of Quantitative Detection of Phenylethylamine.

head.ce:title: Mechano-thermochemical synthesis of chiral multicentre fluorescent MOFs for chiral VOCs detection and adsorption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in wastewater pose a serious threat to human health and the environment. With the increasing demand for chiral pharmaceuticals and the development of the chiral industry, the identification, detection and removal of chiral VOCs has become an urgent challenge. Here, we have constructed chiral fluorescent metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) composites for the first time using single chiral amino acids as a chiral source and in situ carbonation of precursors, integrating both chiral and heterologous multiluminescent centres in the composite system. The blue-green-red luminescence system is composed of heterologous multi-luminescent centres, i.e. ligands, carbon quantum dots and rare earth metals. This heterologous nature allows efficient and accurate qualitative identification of VOCs species and quantitative detection of chiral VOCs concentrations. In particular, its monochiral structure enables enantiomeric excess values detection of chiral enantiomeric mixtures. In addition, the solvent-free synthesis process with competitive coordination of amino acids successfully constructed the multilevel pore structure of the composites, which effectively increased the adsorption rate of the materials by 30 times. The present work provides an efficient method to introduce chirality into multi-centre luminescent MOFs systems, and provides a guiding direction and material design ideas for chiral pollutant detection and removal technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are one of the major sources of environmental pollution with serious impacts on both human health and the environment. VOCs in groundwater can be extremely harmful at certain concentrations and come from a variety of sources. They can be introduced indirectly through gas-water exchange from petrol engine exhaust, industrial waste gases or directly from landfills, municipal waste water or waste water from the pharmaceutical industry[1]. For environmental assessment and remediation, it is essential that VOCs can be detected quickly and easily and removed effectively from water.

In the pharmaceutical industry, more than half of the drug molecules are chiral compounds, and enantiomers usually show different pharmacological and toxicological effects. Chiral small molecules are widely used as synthetic raw materials or enantiomeric splitters, and the presence of potential chiral VOCs in industrial wastewater is inevitable [2]. The qualitative identification and quantitative detection of chiral VOCs in wastewater are gaining attention due to the necessity and increasing scale of pharmaceutical production. Although gas chromatography is currently one of the more mature techniques for the detection of chiral VOCs, there is an urgent need to propose more portable sensing devices or techniques considering the complexity of the environment and conditions [3].

Chiral fluorescence sensing is an emerging technology for efficient, convenient, and visualised detection of chiral molecules. Chiral metal-organic frameworks (CMOFs) have attracted much attention due to their inherent porosity, tunable structure, multiple luminescent centres and domain-limited encapsulation capabilities, especially their monochiral nature for enantioselective recognition. Shi et al. successfully synthesized chiral-deficient MOFs for chiral selective sensing by using a chiral molecule-partial substitution for a ligand strategy as shown in Fig. 1a [4]. However, these single-centre fluorescent sensing materials have poor anti-interference ability, are easily affected by a number of factors such as instrument state, test environment, and operating conditions, and are unable to detect the type and concentration of substances simultaneously. In this regard, the construction of multi-centre fluorescent sensing materials provides an effective approach to solve these problems. At present, fluorescence detection of VOCs containing chiral enantiomers is still at the stage of single emission detection [5–8]. The integration of chiral functionality with multi-emission centres involving more complex introduction of functional units and more tedious synthesis steps is less explored. According to the structural characteristics of MOFs, the luminescent centres can be divided into metal ions/clusters, organic ligands and guest substances [9]. Carbon dots (CDs) are considered to be an excellent luminescent guest due to the advantages of stability, biocompatibility, low cost, small size, and synergistic interaction with the main body of MOFs [10,11]. In classical solvothermal synthesis, the means of combining MOFs with CDs is mainly to add pre-synthesised CDs to the synthetic system of MOFs, so as to construct a composite system of MOFs with domain-limited CDs, as shown in Fig. 1b [12]. In order to simplify this compounding process, we have previously succeeded in the one-step synthesis of CDs@MOFs complexes using quaternary ammonium salts as additives. In addition, rare earth ions have excellent luminescence properties as photoresponsive metal nodes using "antenna effect" sensitisation and 4f-4f energy level jumps, which have been extensively studied in the last two decades [13]. Both are high quality optional heterogeneous luminescent centres, which means that the excitation spectra and pathways are different. The heterogeneous nature allows them to respond differently to different types of substances.

Achieving contaminant removal along with detection is a convenient and efficient means of environmental remediation. Compared to uniform porosity MOFs, hierarchical porous MOFs offer advantages in terms of improved mass transfer efficiency, increased specific surface area and more active sites, which are particularly important in macromolecular or highly viscous systems, implying higher mass transfer rates and more adsorption sites for contaminant adsorption [14,15]. The synthesis of high-purity materials by bottom-up construction of hierarchical porous structures in a template-free form is straightforward and efficient. High concentrations of precursors are considered to be an excellent method for rapid nucleation. Therefore, mechanochemical or derivatisation methods are seen as an efficient way to build hierarchical porous structures.

Herein, a mechano-thermochemical (MT) approach, i.e. mechanochemistry combined with a heat treatment process, was used to successfully integrate chiral and multiluminescent centres into MOFs composites. The chiral composite MOFs system, MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, was successfully constructed by using chiral amino acids as the chiral source and precursor for the synthesis of carbon dots and UiO-66, which is known for its stability, as the main body of the framework and doped with Eu3 + in the metal clusters. The in situ carbonation of amino acids to CDs in the main body of the MOFs and the engineering of defects to construct the multilevel pores were confirmed by the material characterisation. The composite CDs&Eu@UiO-66 contains ligands, carbon dots and rare earth ions as blue, green and red luminescent centres, respectively. The combination of monochiral amino acids introduces chiral structures for qualitative identification classification and quantitative detection of non-chiral/chiral VOCs. In particular, the MOFs composites constructed by solvent-free synthesis have a hierarchical pore structure, which effectively enhances the adsorption efficiency and adsorption capacity of VOCs.
All raw materials and reagents used in this experiment were commercially available, analytically pure reagents. No further purification was required.

The route for the solvent-free synthesis of MT-UiO-66 was improved by the mechano-thermochemical synthesis process. First, ZrOCl2·8 H2O (1 mmol) and H2BDC (terephthalic acid, 1 mmol) were mixed and ground at room temperature for 5 min. The resulting material was then transferred to a 25 mL autoclave and crystallised at 180 °C for 24 h. The product was cooled to room temperature and washed several times with ethanol. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected and washed several times with ethanol and then dried at 80 °C for 10 h to obtain MT-UiO-66.

The route for the solvent-free synthesis of MT-Eu@UiO-66 was similar to that for MT-UiO-66. First, ZrOCl2·8 H2O (0.9 mmol), Eu(NO3)3·6 H2O (0.1 mmol) and H2BDC (1 mmol) were mixed and ground at room temperature for 5 min, followed by heat treatment, washing and drying steps as for MT-UiO-66.

The route for the solvent-free synthesis of MT-CDs@UiO-66 was similar to that for MT-UiO-66. First, ZrOCl2·8 H2O (1 mmol), monochiral phenylalanine (L-Phe or D-Phe, 1 mmol) and H2BDC (1 mmol) were mixed and ground at room temperature for 5 min. The subsequent heat treatment, washing and drying steps were the same as for MT-CDs@UiO-66.

The solvent-free synthesis of MT-Eu&CDs@UiO-66 (L-Phe or D-Phe) was similar to that of MT-UiO-66. First, ZrOCl2·8 H2O (0.9 mmol), Eu(NO3)3·6 H2O (0.1 mmol), monochiral phenylalanine (L-Phe or D-Phe, 1 mmol) and H2BDC (1 mmol) were mixed and ground at room temperature for 5 min. The subsequent heat treatment, washing and drying steps were the same as for MT-UiO-66 to obtain MT-Eu&CDs@UiO-66 (L-Phe or D-Phe). For MT-Eu&CDs@UiO-66, unless otherwise indicated in the text, L-Phe was used in the synthesis.

The solvent-free synthesis of MT-CDs@ZiF-8 was similar to that described above. Firstly, dimethylimidazole (2.5-X mmol), L-histidine (L-His, X mmol, X = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 or 1 mmol), Zn(CH3COO)2 (1 mmol) and NaOH (1 mmol) were mixed and ground for 5 min and then transferred to an autoclave for crystallisation at 70 °C for 24 hours. The resulting solid was washed several times with pure water and ethanol and dried under vacuum at 60 °C to obtain MT-CDs@ZiF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative detection of VOCs in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of 14 VOCs (i.e., methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, acetone, 1,4-dioxane, tetrahydrofuran, cyclohexane, o-xylene, m-xylene, p-xylene, (R)-1-phenylethanol, (S)-1-phenylethanol, (S)-1-phenylethylamine, (R)-1-phenylethylamine) on the sensing performance of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 aqueous solution was investigated. 3 mg of CDs&Eu@UiO-66 was dissolved in 5 mL of water and sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous suspension. VOCs were then added to the above solution at a volume ratio of VOCs to water of 1 %. After sonication for a further 0.5 h and leaving for 12 h, the treated MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 solution was subjected to a fluorescence assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of chiral VOCs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Quantifying aqueous solutions of chiral volatile organic compounds using MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 was investigated. 5 mg of CDs&Eu@UiO-66 was dissolved in 5 mL of water and sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous suspension. VOCs were then added to the above solution at VOC/water volume ratios of 0, 0.1 %, 0.2 %, 0.4 %, 0.8 %, 1.6 %, 3.2 %, 6.4 % and 12.8 %. After sonication for a further 0.5 h and a 12 h standing time, the treated MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 solution was subjected to a fluorescence test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the ee values of chiral enantiomeric mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg of CDs&Eu@UiO-66 was dissolved in 5 mL of water and sonicated for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous suspension. VOCs were then added to the above solution with a volume ratio of enantiomeric mixture to water of 2 % and ee of (+)-PEA of −100 %, −60 %, −20 %, 20 %, 60 % and 100 % respectively. After further sonication for 0.5 h and leaving for 12 h, the treated MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 solution was tested for fluorescence.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were collected using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å) on a Rigaku D/MAX 2500 X-ray diffractometer with voltages and currents of 40 kV and 200 mA, respectively, and 2θ in the range of 2–60°, with a scanning speed of 5°/min. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained from a Hitachi SU-8010, and elemental mapping of the samples was obtained from an accompanying energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the samples were taken using a JEM-F200 field emission transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of powders were collected by a Bruker α FTIR-ATR instrument (Germany) with a resolution value of 4 cm−1, scan time of 16 min, and wavenumber ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Analysis of surface area and pore structure was carried out by N2 adsorption-desorption on the Autosorb-IQ2, calculated using the Brunner-Emmett-Taylor (BET) method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of solid powders and dispersions were studied on an FLS 1000 steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as excitation light source. UV-Vis spectra data were recorded using a Shimadzu UV-1800 UV/Visible scanning spectrophotometer. Circularly polarised luminescence (CPL) spectra were measured by JASCO CPL-300. 1H (400 MHz) NMR spectra of samples in H2D were recorded on an AVANCE III 400 spectrometer.

The MT-MOFs composites synthesised by mechano-thermochemical methods and their control samples were structurally and morphologically characterised to assess the crystallinity of the MOF body, the carbon point and framework composite structure, and the pore properties of the composites. The successful incorporation of CDs and rare earth ions Eu into the framework body UiO-66 was confirmed. The measured powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns, as shown in Fig. 2a, indicate that the composites obtained by the solvent-free route still maintain a high and good crystallinity of the UiO-66 framework compared to the classical ST-UiO-66 (ST, solvothermal) synthesised by the comparative solvothermal method [16]. In particular, MT-Eu@UiO-66, MT-CDs@UiO-66 (D-Phe), MT-CDs@UiO-66 (L-Phe) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (L-Phe), synthesised by doping with elemental Eu or/and monochiral phenylalanine (Phe), exhibit good crystallinity in agreement with the classical UiO-66 profiles. This demonstrates the advantages of the UiO-66 composite body as a platform for the integration of functionalities with a well-defined structure and a high degree of purity.

The morphology of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 and the complexation of CDs with the main body of MOFs were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2b, SEM showed that the resulting MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 had good dispersion and relatively homogeneous morphology, with particle diameters in the range of 30–40 nm. TEM images further revealed that spherical CDs with diameters in the range of 5–10 nm were dispersed in the matrix of MOFs, and two types of lattices, 0.21 nm and 0.31 nm, were observed with distinct stripe spacing (Fig. 2c), corresponding to the (100) and (001) crystal planes of the graphite phase, respectively [11]. This demonstrates the in situ generation of carbon nanocrystals, i.e. CDs, inside the MOF particles. In addition, ST-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 was used as a comparison sample for ion doping and addition of amino acids (Phe) by solvothermal synthesis, and the TEM images showed that no CDs appeared in the main body of the MOFs (Fig. S1). The results demonstrate the advantage of a solvent-free environment for the generation of CDs.

Comparison of the FTIR spectra of mechano-thermochemically synthesised MOFs, classical solvent-thermally synthesised UiO-66, and the raw material, the mechano-thermochemically synthesised MT-UiO-66 and its composite system showed characteristic absorption peaks similar to those of classical ST-UiO-66. Characteristically, the absorption peaks are attributed to O-C-O asymmetry and symmetric stretching in the terephthalic acid ligand at 1580 and 1395 cm−1. In a slightly different way, the free -COOH harmonic vibrational modes in the MT-MOFs system are all present at 1673 cm−1, labelled in the yellow part of Fig. 2d. Meanwhile, the characteristic absorption peaks from O-H bonds are all present at 3000–3500 cm−1, labelled in the green part in Fig. 2d. This is attributed to the uninvolved carboxyl groups of the ligand in the solvent-free environment pathway. The significant 'defective engineering' feature demonstrates the superiority of the solvent-free environment in the construction of defective MOFs systems [17].

To further investigate the effect of the above 'defect engineering' on the porosity of the composites, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption method was used to evaluate ST-UiO-66, ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66. The specific surface areas of the samples were calculated and the pore size distributions were analysed using the Brunner-Emmett-Taylor (BET) model and the Non-Local Density Flooding Theory (NLDFT), respectively, as shown in Fig. 2e, f. The measured specific surface areas of ST-UiO-66, ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 were 1302.86, 1043.43 and 794.45 m2⋅g−1, respectively. It is known that the porosity of the porous material decreases with the addition of metal references and amino acids. It is noteworthy that the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 show a combination of type I and type III isotherms and also, in Fig. 2f, the appearance of mesoporous distribution in the range of 2–10 nm in addition to micropores [15]. This indicates the coexistence of microporous and mesoporous pore structures in the system without solvent and amino acid participation. The combination of FTIR and BET pore size analysis shows that the solvent-free environment induces the 'defect engineering' and preserves part of the unbonded carboxyl groups, which constructs the hierarchical pore structure of the MOFs composite system, and this will indicate that this functionally integrated platform will have a great advantage in improving the sensing and adsorption efficiencies.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigations were carried out on ST-UiO-66 and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 to clarify the compositions and valence states of Eu, CDs and UiO-66 frameworks after compounding. Fig. 2g shows the full survey of ST-UiO-66 (top) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (bottom). ST-UiO-66 and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 consists of Zr, Eu, N, C and O, as shown in Fig. 2h-l. The fitted Eu 3d spectra in MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 obtained peaks at 1124.4 eV, 1153.9 eV, and 1165.5 eV in Fig. 2i, proving that Eu was successfully doped in the composites, while it was not in ST-UiO-66. The C1s spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, shown in Fig. 2j, were fitted to three types of C, i.e, C-C/CC (sp2 C, 284.2 eV), C-O/C-N (sp3 C, 285.8 eV), and -CO (288.3 eV).The percentage of C-C/CC (sp2 C) in MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 increases, compared to ST-UiO-66, that was attributed to the formation of CDs (graphitic phase C) in the composite [18]. The N 1 s spectra of MT-CDs & Eu@UiO-66 can be fitted to pyrrolic N (399.0 eV) and graphitic N/N-H (401.1 eV) respectively, further demonstrating the formation of graphitic phase CDs in Fig. 2l [19], and the weaker N-(C)3 (400.0 eV) in ST-UiO-66 is attributed to trace nitrogen-containing solvent residues [20]. The XPS of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 prepared by solvent-free method is different compared to that of ST-UiO-66, and the differences are mainly shown in the Eu 3d, C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s spectra. The solvent-free environment contributes to the partial carbonation of monochiral amino acids, which leads to a significant increase in graphite C/N content. In addition, due to the low diffusion effect of the solvent-free environment, some of the uncarbonated amino acids competed with the ligands for coordination with the Zr/Eu metal-oxygen clusters, contributing to the subtle changes in the O 1 s spectra. (Fig. S13)

The photoluminescence properties of solvent-free synthesised MT-MOFs were investigated by steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy. The luminescence mechanism of each luminescence centre was elucidated to provide a theoretical and application basis for the specific identification and detection of VOCs. To demonstrate the rationality of the progressive composite route, the solid-state photoluminescence behaviours of MT-UiO-66, MT-CDs@UiO-66, MT-Eu@UiO-66 and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 are analysed and compared. The solvent-free synthesised MT-UiO-66 exhibits ligand-derived blue emission under 310 nm excitation and its fluorescence excitation and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 3a [21]. Notably, the luminescence properties of MT-CDs@UiO-66 with only the addition of amino acid (Phe) showed the excitation-dependent characteristic emission behaviour of carbon dots, i.e. the emission light changed from blue-green to green and then to yellow as the excitation wavelength changed from 360 nm to 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 3b. This further supports the in situ carbonylation of amino acids into CDs in the solvent-free synthesis process [22]. The Eu-metal doped mixed metal MOFs, MT-Eu@UiO-66, exhibited weaker 350–410 nm broad band emission of the ligand and stronger characteristic narrow band emission of Eu3+. The five characteristic emission peaks correspond to the 5D0-7F0, 5D0-7F1, 5D0-7F2, 5D0-7F3 and 5D0-7F4 energy level jumps originating from Eu3+, respectively [23,24]. The corresponding positions of the fluorescence emission of MT-UiO-66, MT-CDs@UiO-66 and MT-Eu@UiO-66 are shown in the CIE (x, y) coordinate diagram as in Fig. 3f. In the further composite MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, the emission profile of the composite changes dramatically as the excitation wavelength changes from 240 nm to 500 nm, i.e. blue-red-green-yellow, as shown in Fig. 3d. Specifically, it is the blue emission of the ligand at 388 nm that is dominant at an excitation wavelength of 240 nm. As the excitation wavelength is red-shifted, the ligand emission is attenuated while the rare earth ion emission is enhanced and the emission spectrum mainly shows the characteristic narrow band red emission of the rare earth ion Eu3+. If the excitation wavelength is further shifted beyond 310 nm, the rare earth ion emission is attenuated and the green emission from the carbon dots is predominant. Due to the inherent excitation-dependent behaviour of the carbon dots, as the excitation wavelength is further increased, the fluorescence of the composite is gradually red-shifted from green emission to yellow emission. Photographs of the composite at 254 nm, 302 nm and 365 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, are shown in Fig. 3e. The variation of the excitation-dependent emission of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 is shown in the CIE (x, y) coordinate diagram, Fig. 3g, while the excitation spectra corresponding to different emission centres are shown in Fig. S2. The composites have different excitation spectra corresponding to multiple emission centres, i.e. different photoluminescence mechanisms. This results in multiple fluorescence response behaviours to different external stimuli, providing strong support for subsequent qualitative and quantitative detection [21]. Satisfactorily, the chiral signal in the composite material MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 is transmitted from a single chiral amino acid to a rare earth ion, which presents as a strong circularly polarised luminescence (CPL), as shown in Fig. 4. The asymmetry factors glum of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (D-Phe) and MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (L-Phe) are 5.97 × 10−3 and 6.33 × 10−3, respectively, which give better performance among the chiral MOFs [25–27]. This demonstrates the introduction of chiral structures in the composite material to provide support for the differential response of chiral enantiomers. In addition, it is demonstrated that the material has excellent prospects for applications in the encryption and storage of information [28].

The relative independence of individual emission peaks due to different luminescence mechanisms in the heterogeneous multiple luminescence centre composite, MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, offers the possibility of selective sensing. At the same time, the multiple luminescent centres allow self-referencing to achieve highly accurate signals without external interferences such as concentration, excitation light source, detector state, etc [29]. In order to evaluate the sensing performance of the composite material CDs&Eu@UiO-66, fluorescence response tests were performed on solutions of 14 VOCs. Fig. 5a lists the structural formulae and names of the VOCs, including two groups of chiral VOCs. The fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions corresponding to the different VOCs, shown in Fig. 5b, were normalised using the emission peak originating from the carbon point (504 nm) as an internal reference. The corresponding UV photographs are shown in Fig. 5c, irradiated from top to bottom with excitation wavelengths of 254 nm, 302 nm and 365 nm, respectively. The emission peaks originating from the ligand (338 nm) and metal Eu (616 nm) from the PL spectrum were plotted as shown in Fig. 5d. Analysis of the fluorescence spectra as well as changes in the visualisation of the dispersions showed that VOCs without benzene ring structure, such as methanol, ethanol, tetrahydrofuran and cyclohexane, had no significant effect on the dispersion emission, and that VOCs with internal benzene ring structure burst the red emission of the rare earth metal Eu in the composites, with the chiral VOC phenylethylamine being the most obvious for the red light burst phenomenon. Specifically, the basic phenethylamine not only causes the red fluorescence of the rare earth ion to burst, but also leads to the phenomenon of ligand blue fluorescence enhancement, compared to the green fluorescence of the carbon dot. Notably, for the chiral enantiomers, the emission profile of the composite MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 reacts with different degrees. As shown in Fig. 5b, d, at the same concentration, the composite system has a stronger burst for R-phenylethylamine with more enhanced ligand fluorescence emission compared to S-phenylethylamine. A similar differential response profile was observed for the chiral enantiomer phenylethanol. Such an efficient and easy way to discriminate the unknown VOCs solution provides a convenient detection supplement for the detection of unknown substances in the process of drug production and environmental control.

To evaluate the sensing sensitivity of the materials, photoluminescence tests were performed on the composite solution system in different solubilities of phenethylamine solution as shown in Fig. 6a,b. The statistics of their fluorescence spectral changes are shown in Fig. 6c. It can be seen that the red light emission of the rare earth ion Eu3+ gradually decreases, and the blue fluorescence of the ligand is enhanced as the concentration of phenethylamine increases. The fluorescence intensity shows a good linear relationship with the logarithm of the phenylethylamine concentration (log10(Vs/Vm)) of the detected substance, which means that the material can be used as a qualified phenylethylamine detector. Quantitatively, the linear relationship between the emission intensities I 388 nm and I 616 nm (self-referential carbon point green emission) the volume ratio of phenylethylamine to water over the range of 0–12.8 % is shown in Table 1 below.

Due to the chiral properties of the composite system, the material has a different response to the chiral enantiomers. Therefore, the intensity variation of the composite system, i.e. the enantiomeric excess (ee) value of the enantiomeric mixture, is shown in Fig. 6d,e. The ee value is defined as (a - b)/(a + b) × 100 %, where a and b denote volume concentrations of R and S enantiomers of phenylethylamine, respectively. The luminescence spectra of the composites were measured with the addition of different proportions of the enantiomeric mixtures (Fig. 6d). The normalized emission intensities I 388 nm and I 616 nm, relative to I 504 nm, showed a good linear relationship with the ee of R-phenylethylamine in the tested range (Fig. 6e). These results indicate that the composites are effective enantiomeric sensing materials for the detection of ee values of mixed chiral volatile organic compounds. This leads to three hypotheses for investigating the sensing mechanism of the MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 composite platform, i.e. the mechanism causing the fluorescence burst/enhancement: (1) The composite material is damaged and the structure collapses; (2) Phenylethylamine interacts with Eu3+; (3) Interaction between phenylethylamine and the ligand. As shown in Fig. S3, after sensing by the phenylethylamine solution system, the PXRD pattern of the composite material MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 is basically the same as that of the original PXRD. Therefore, it is inferred that the change in fluorescence of the composite solution is not caused by the structural collapse of the material. The LUMO energy levels of the ligand terephthalic acid (donor) calculated from density functional theory (DFT) calculations are significantly lower than those of phenethylamine (acceptor), as shown in Table S1, and there is no effective photoinduced electron transfer (PET) in this system [30]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of VOCs containing benzene ring structures, such as phenethylamine, partially overlapped with the excitation spectra of CDs&Eu@UiO-66 (Fig. 6f). Therefore, absorption competition (AC) of the excitation light occurs between CDs&Eu@UiO-66 and the analyte during the detection process. There is no overlap between the UV-Vis absorption spectrum and the emission spectrum of CDs&Eu@UiO-66, indicating the absence of the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [4,30]. Together with the AC process, the analyte interferes with the energy transfer from the ligand to the rare-earth ions, i.e. it interferes with the 'antenna effect', resulting in the attenuation of the rare-earth ion radiative forcing. The above suggests that the rare earth metal dominated red light burst of the composite is affected by AC. In addition, phenylethylamine has a special characteristic compared to other VOCs, i.e. it has an amino functional group that can interact with the rare earth metal Eu, which may lead to the strong rare earth fluorescence burst in composites. To compare aspects in the sensor system, the blue fluorescence enhancement response of the ligand is actually the green fluorescence decrease of the carbon dots [31]. This is due to the protonation-deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots in the alkaline solution provided by phenylethylamine. The differential response of the chiral enantiomers was attributed to the selective adsorption of the monochiral system, and this differential adsorption result was further verified in later adsorption experiments. We discuss in detail the sensing mechanism of chiral VOCs in the analysed system in terms of the construction of chiral structures and differential adsorption in the Supporting Information. (Fig. S13) In summary, the visualised response behaviour of the composite system provides an opportunity for efficient sensing and the enantioselective recognition and provides a new method for rapid detection of ee values of enantiomeric mixtures.

The hierarchical pore structure provided by the solvent-free synthesis route offers potential for efficient adsorption of large molecular contaminants [15]. In order to investigate the adsorption performance of the MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 composite, the dye Rhodamine B, which has a large molecular size, was used as a model for the dynamic adsorption experiments, and ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe) and ST-UiO-66 were used as reference samples. Photographs of the colour change of the dye solution at different adsorption times are shown in Fig. 7a-c. Correspondingly, the UV–visible spectra of the solutions are shown in Fig. 7d-f. A more visual comparison of the adsorption efficiencies is shown in Fig. 7g. Within 5 min, MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 adsorbed to remove 88 % of the dye, which improved the adsorption rate by about 30 times and 3 times compared to ST-UiO-66 and ST-CDs&Eu@UiO-66, respectively. In addition, the adsorption capacity of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 composites for rhodamine B was also significantly increased. Therefore, the solvent-free synthesised MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 has a faster adsorption rate and larger adsorption capacity for rhodamine B compared to the reference samples synthesised by solvothermal method. The great improvement in the adsorption rate is mainly attributed to the acceleration of the mass transfer of the adsorption process by the multistage pores. The increase in adsorption capacity is attributed to the increase in uncoordinated functional groups, as shown by the FTIR of the materials in Fig. 1d, resulting from the solvent-free crystallisation process in a high supersaturation environment and the presence of amino acids. The abundance of functional groups readily formed hydrogen bonds with macromolecular organic dyes, providing a higher dye removal rate for the solvent-free synthesised composites. To investigate the removal performance of chiral VOCs, enantiomeric adsorption experiments of phenylethylamine were carried out as shown in Fig. 7h. The adsorption amount of R-phenylethylamine was up to 28.9 mg/g and that of s-phenylethylamine up to 27.0 mg/g. Combined with the kinetic model equations fitted to the experimental adsorption kinetics data, it was basically in agreement with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model curves (Fig. 7i), indicating that it is a chemical adsorption mechanism for phenylethylamine. It is noteworthy that the composites showed selective adsorption capacity for the chiral enantiomers, which was mainly due to the the introduced single chiral amino acid. It is further argued that the composites have successfully inherited the chirality of the amino acids.

To explore the realisation of this chiral amino acid carbonation in other MOFs frameworks, the chiral CDs@MOFs composite system was reconstructed using the classical porous ZIF-8 as the main body of the new framework and the monochiral L-histidine (L-His) as the chiral source and carbon point precursor [32,33]. With different amounts of L-histidine added, MT-CDs@ZIF-8 showed a trend of weakening crystallinity, as shown in Fig. 8a. The fluorescence emission spectra showed distinct carbon dot emission peaks, as shown in Fig. 8b. The optimal fluorescence intensity corresponds to the L-His addition of 20 %, at which point the main body of the composite retains a high degree of crystallinity. The successful composite of CDs with ZIF-8 further demonstrates the universality of the method of in situ carbonation of single chiral amino acid in the main body framework of MOFs, which provides an effective way to construct an integrated system of chiral and multiluminescent centres. The idea of integrating chiral and multiluminescent centres will promote the rapid development of more complex systems such as chiral recognition and chiral catalysis [34].

Notably, mechano-thermochemical synthesis is extremely space-efficient as a solvent-free route. For example, the composite MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 achieves a space-time yield (SPY) of 1623 kg·m−3·d−1, almost 10 times that of the solvent-thermal synthesis [35]. And the SPY of MT-CDs@ZIF-8 reaches 1547 kg·m−3·d−1. The high SPY, basic equipment and simple route make these chiral functional composites easy to industrialise on a large scale.

In conclusion, an efficient and environmentally friendly solvent-free route is proposed that integrates heterologous multiluminescent centres with chiral structures. Qualitative recognition and quantitative detection of VOCs containing chiral enantiomers were successfully achieved with this integrated system and demonstrated in chiral enantiomeric phenethylamines. At the same time, the solvent-free environment and the hierarchical pore structure facilitated by competitive amino acid coordination achieved an order of magnitude increase in the adsorption rate of the porous material. This strategy of integrating chiral and heterologous multiluminescent centres will play a crucial role in the production and detection process of chiral drugs and chiral pesticides. The hierarchical pore property provides the composite system the advantages of rapid response and efficient removal in the field of contaminant detection and control. In addition, chirality, multiluminescent centres, multilevel pores and other structural and functional integration pathways greatly facilitate the preparation of chiral materials and the development of chiral chemistry and chemical industries. In the future, tailoring the chiral pore size for de-adaptive sensing of specific chiral enantiomers is even more attractive. In addition, the ease of scale-up and the green chemistry properties of solvent-free synthesis offer advantages in terms of environmental protection and low-cost production. Our group is actively investigating both of these aspects.

Experimental details of the solvothermal synthesis of ST-UiO-66 and ST-Eu@UiO-66 (Phe). The TEM images of solvothermally synthesised ST-CDs&Eu@UiO-66. The XRD fluorescence excitation spectra of MT-CDs&Eu@UiO-66 after the sensing. HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the ligands and analytes. Comparative sensing tests. Analysis of the chiral sensing mechanism. (PDF).

Su Xin: Data curation. Xu Yan: Writing – review & editing. Liu Jiaqiang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tang Weiwei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Gong Junbo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Journal of the American Chemical Society 137 (2015) 1572-1580. https://doi.org/10.1021/ja511539a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: ZIF-8: a comparison of synthesis methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lee, Y.-R., Jang, M.-S., Cho, H.-Y., Kwon, H.-J., Kim, S., Ahn, W.-S., ZIF-8: A comparison of synthesis methods. Chemical Engineering Journal 271 (2015) 276-280. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2015.02.094.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral recognition and separation by chirality-enriched metal-organic frameworks

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das, S., Xu, S., Ben, T., Qiu, S., Chiral Recognition and Separation by Chirality-Enriched Metal-Organic Frameworks. Angewandte Chemie International Edition 57 (2018) 8629-8633. https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201804383.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improved continuous synthesis of UiO-66 enabling outstanding production rates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Polyzoidis, A., Reichle, S., Schwarzer, M., Piscopo, C.G., Löbbecke, S., Boskovic, D., 2021. Improved continuous synthesis of UiO-66 enabling outstanding production rates. Reaction Chemistry & Engineering 6(4), 679-684. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0re00464b.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Liu Jiaqiang is a PhD candidate at the School of Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University. His research interest is the development of crystalline functional materials by solvent-free synthesis techniques.
Xin Su is a PhD candidate at the School of Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University.

Yan Xu is a professor at the School of Science, Northeastern University (China). She received his PhD from Jilin University (2013). Her main research interests include the functionalized assembly of host-guest nanoporous materials and their luminescence and optoelectronic sensing properties.

Weiwei Tang is an Associate Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University. He received his PhD from Tianjin University and Purdue University (joint training, 2017). His main research interests include industrial crystallisation, biomineralisation and chiral splitting.

Junbo Gong is a professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University. He received his PhD from Tianjin University (2001) and was a postdoctoral fellow at the University of Michigan School of Pharmacy (2006–2008).
